description: HMRC is the UK’s tax, payments and customs authority, and we have a vital purpose: we collect the money that pays for the UK’s public services, help families and individuals with targeted financial support and provide the property valuations that support taxation and benefits. We do this by being impartial and increasingly effective and efficient in our administration. We help the honest majority to get their tax right and make it hard for the dishonest minority to cheat the system. HMRC is a non-ministerial department, supported by 1 public body .

details.body:
HMRC is the UK’s tax, payments and customs authority, and we have a vital purpose: we collect the money that pays for the UK’s public services, help families and individuals with targeted financial support and provide the property valuations that support taxation and benefits.

We do this by being impartial and increasingly effective and efficient in our administration. We help the honest majority to get their tax right and make it hard for the dishonest minority to cheat the system.

HMRC is a non-ministerial department, supported by 1 public body.

details.body:
HM Revenue and Customs’ (HMRC) First Permanent Secretary and Chief Executive chairs the Executive Committee, and is responsible for delivering the department’s strategy, business objectives and performance.

They are the Principal Accounting Officer and are accountable to Parliament for the department’s expenditure and performance.

role.title: First Permanent Secretary and Chief Executive

role_appointments.title: John-Paul Marks - First Permanent Secretary and Chief Executive

details.body: The Permanent Secretary is responsible for the overall management of the department.

role.title: Permanent Secretary for the Scottish Government

role_appointments.title: John-Paul Marks - Permanent Secretary for the Scottish Government

details.body:
The Director General, Work and Health Services is responsible for:

  • the day to day to running of disability services, Universal Credit and working age operations across Great Britain via jobcentres and service centres
  • the provision of all working age face-to-face services through jobcentres, including the relationship with employers and other external partners
  • the delivery of services across the lifecycle of UK households, including child maintenance and pensioners

role.title: Director General, Work and Health Services

role_appointments.title: John-Paul Marks - Director General, Work and Health Services

details.body:
The Director General, Universal Credit Operations is responsible for:

  • the day to day to running of Universal Credit operations across Great Britain via jobcentres and service centres
  • the provision of all working age face-to-face services through jobcentres, including the relationship with employers and other external partners

role_appointments.title: John Paul Marks - Director General, Universal Credit Operations

details.body:
The Deputy Chief Executive and Second Permanent Secretary leads 50,000 colleagues in HMRC who deliver customer service and compliance and enforcement activity across all taxes. In addition, the Deputy Chief Executive is accountable for the transformation and change programmes which ensure the implementation of government policy and a modern, digital tax system.

The Deputy Chief Executive is a Tax Commissioner and accountable to Parliament for the effective and efficient running of HMRC.

role.title: Deputy Chief Executive and Second Permanent Secretary

role_appointments.title: Angela MacDonald - Deputy Chief Executive and Second Permanent Secretary

details.body:
The Director General Customer Services has overall responsibility for delivering consistency in HMRC’s customer services and for providing customers with the joined-up support they expect, across all channels, and in ways that best suit them.

The Customer Services Group manages most of HMRC’s interaction with individual taxpayers, tax credit recipients, and small and mid-sized businesses.

role_appointments.title: Angela MacDonald - Director General Customer Services

details.body:
The Director General Customer Services has overall responsibility for delivering consistency in HMRC’s customer services and for providing customers with the joined-up support they expect, across all channels, and in ways that best suit them.

The Customer Services Group manages most of HMRC’s interaction with individual taxpayers, tax credit recipients, and small and mid-sized businesses.

role_appointments.title: Myrtle Lloyd - Director General Customer Services

details.body:
The Director General Customer Compliance focuses on closing the tax gap to align with our strategic objectives by promoting voluntary compliance, preventing non-compliance and tackling those who cheat the system.

The Customer Compliance Group includes risk and intelligence, compliance for all customer groups, including large business, wealthy people, mid-sized businesses, small businesses and individuals, and HMRC’s counter–avoidance and fraud investigation functions.

role_appointments.title: Penny Ciniewicz - Director General Customer Compliance

details.body:
The Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design has overall responsibility for a group of teams that use customer insight to design better tax strategy, assurance, policies, products and processes that:

  • meet customer needs
  • make it easier for them to get things right
  • reduce the risk of honest mistakes and opportunities to dodge tax

The Director General will also advise ministers on UK tax policy, and work internationally to improve the global tax framework, while producing the strategy for all of HMRC’s core functions, not just its tax operation.

role.title: Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design

role_appointments.title: Jonathan Athow - Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design

details.body:
HMRC’s Chief Digital and Information Officer (CDIO) is responsible for all of the technology powering one of the biggest digitally-enabled transformations anywhere in Europe.

The CDIO leads HMRC’s digital and data strategies and has significant involvement in the cross-government agenda. The CDIO is also responsible for running one of the biggest and most complex IT estates in the UK, and implementing HMRC’s pioneering IT Sourcing Strategy.

The CDIO is the Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO) which covers all aspects of protective, physical, personnel and information security.

role_appointments.title: Daljit Rehal - Chief Digital and Information Officer

details.body: HMRC’s Tax Assurance Commissioner oversees and provides assurance of large tax settlements. The Commissioner oversees the assurance and dispute governance arrangements that allow Parliament and the public to be confident that HMRC secures the right tax under the law when resolving tax disputes. The Commissioner does not directly engage with taxpayers to discuss their specific tax liabilities, nor are they responsible for the HMRC operational units that manage taxpayers’ compliance.

role_appointments.title: Justin Holliday - Tax Assurance Commissioner

details.body: The Chief Finance Officer is responsible for ensuring the highest standards of financial management within HMRC and leading the Commercial, Strategy, Banking, Risk, Control, Accounting, Assurance, Investment and Internal Audit Directorates. Justin has overall responsibility for HMRC’s finance profession.

role_appointments.title: Justin Holliday - Chief Finance Officer

details.body: The Interim Chief Finance Officer is responsible for ensuring the highest standards of financial management within the Department and leading the Commercial, Estates and Support Services, Corporate Finance, Government Banking, Change Assurance & Investment and Internal Audit directorates.

role_appointments.title: Justin Holliday - Interim Chief Finance Officer

details.body:
The Chief People Officer is responsible for the effective and efficient running of the HR Community in HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC). The HR Community have accountability for people management capability and deployment across HMRC, and oversee the delivery of HR services. They are accountable for developing the HMRC People Strategy and the Employer Brand, as well as the delivery of a specialist organisation design service.

The Chief People Officer is also accountable for HMRC’s role as a responsible corporate organisation; and for the governance and delivery of HMRC’s learning and talent activities.

role_appointments.title: Helen Pickles - Chief People Officer

details.body:
The Director General, People and Capability sets and leads the department’s people strategy, aligning this to business strategy and the wider Civil Service workforce plan.

The role is accountable for:

  • the full HR operating model and function
  • health and safety
  • learning and development
  • strategic workforce planning
  • creating a flexible, inclusive and continuously learning workforce

role_appointments.title: Helen Pickles - Director General, People, Capability and Place

details.body:
The Chief People Officer (interim) is responsible for the effective and efficient running of the HR Community in HM Revenue and Customs.

The HR community has accountability for people management capability and deployment across HMRC, and oversees the delivery of HR services.

It is accountable for developing the HMRC People Strategy and the Employer Brand, as well as the delivery of a specialist organisation design service.

The Chief People Officer (interim) is also accountable for HMRC’s role as a responsible corporate organisation and for the governance and delivery of HMRC’s learning and talent activities.

role_appointments.title: Alan Evans - Chief People Officer (interim)

details.body:
The General Counsel and Solicitor is responsible for delivering legal, accountancy, tax review and litigation services to HMRC.

The work of the HMRC Legal Group includes:

  • advising HMRC on all aspects of the law
  • carrying out the department’s civil litigation
  • providing legal and accountancy advice on policy initiatives
  • working on new legislation, such as the Finance Bill and other Bills, and also secondary legislation

role_appointments.title: Alan Evans - General Counsel and Solicitor

details.body:
Non-Executive Directors bring external experience and expertise to the department, playing an important role in providing advice, challenge and scrutiny to the work of the Executive Committee and the department more widely, both within and outside of formal Board and sub-committee meetings.

Non-Executives contribute their expertise outside the formal Board and subcommittee structures — for example, working closely with executives on specific initiatives, mentoring and undertaking in-depth examinations of risk.

Non-Executive Directors also support the effectiveness of programme boards for HMRC’s most significant transformation programmes.

role_appointments.title: Bill Dodwell - Non-Executive Director

details.body: The Tax Director is responsible to Ministers for the Office’s work, including the content of its reports.

role.title: Tax Director, Office of Tax Simplification

role_appointments.title: Bill Dodwell - Tax Director, Office of Tax Simplification

details.body:
The Director General Borders and Trade has overall responsibility for leading and delivering the transformation of post EU Exit border and customs arrangements and facilitating trade with international partners.

The role also has departmental responsibility for delivery of customs revenue at the border, working closely with Border Force.

role.title: Interim Director General Borders and Trade

role_appointments.title: Carol Bristow - Interim Director General Borders and Trade

details.body: The Member of the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration is independent. Its role is to make recommendations to the Prime Minister, the Secretary of State for Health, the First Minister and the Cabinet Secretary for Health and Wellbeing of the Scottish Parliament, the First Minister and the Minister for Health and Social Services in the Welsh Assembly Government and the First Minister, Deputy First Minister and Minister for Health, Social Services and Public Safety of the Northern Ireland Executive on the remuneration of doctors and dentists taking any part in the National Health Service.

details.body:
HM Revenue and Customs’ (HMRC) First Permanent Secretary and Chief Executive chairs the Executive Committee, and is responsible for delivering the department’s strategy, business objectives and performance.

They are the Principal Accounting Officer and are accountable to Parliament for the department’s expenditure and performance.

ordered_roles.title: First Permanent Secretary and Chief Executive

details.body: The Chief Finance Officer is responsible for ensuring the highest standards of financial management within HMRC and leading the Commercial, Strategy, Banking, Risk, Control, Accounting, Assurance, Investment and Internal Audit Directorates. Justin has overall responsibility for HMRC’s finance profession.

The Director General, Enforcement and Compliance has overall responsibility for a group of directorates whose staff ensure that HMRC successfully collects the full and correct amount of money due from UK taxpayers, investigates offences against the tax system and takes action to identify and mitigate potential threats.

HMRC’s Tax Assurance Commissioner oversees and provides assurance of large tax settlements. The Commissioner oversees the assurance and dispute governance arrangements that allow Parliament and the public to be confident that HMRC secures the right tax under the law when resolving tax disputes. The Commissioner does not directly engage with taxpayers to discuss their specific tax liabilities, nor are they responsible for the HMRC operational units that manage taxpayers’ compliance.

The Director General Business Tax is responsible for HMRC’s business tax and duty regimes and the department’s overall performance in relation to business customers, including cross-cutting strategies and programmes for SMEs, agents, employers and large business.
The Director General Benefits and Credits is responsible for strategy and policy as well as operational performance for tax credits, Child Benefit, Guardians Allowance and Tax Free Child Care.

The Chief People Officer is responsible for the effective and efficient running of the HR Community in HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC). The HR Community have accountability for people management capability and deployment across HMRC, and oversee the delivery of HR services. They are accountable for developing the HMRC People Strategy and the Employer Brand, as well as the delivery of a specialist organisation design service.

The Chief People Officer is also accountable for HMRC’s role as a responsible corporate organisation; and for the governance and delivery of HMRC’s learning and talent activities.

The Director General Personal Tax has overall responsibility for policy and service delivery for individual customers (including employees and pensioners), and for PAYE, Self Assessment, National Insurance and other taxes including Inheritance Tax and Capital Gains Tax.

HMRC’s Chief Digital and Information Officer (CDIO) is responsible for all of the technology powering one of the biggest digitally-enabled transformations anywhere in Europe.

The CDIO leads HMRC’s digital and data strategies and has significant involvement in the cross-government agenda. The CDIO is also responsible for running one of the biggest and most complex IT estates in the UK, and implementing HMRC’s pioneering IT Sourcing Strategy.

The CDIO is the Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO) which covers all aspects of protective, physical, personnel and information security.

The Interim Chief Finance Officer is responsible for ensuring the highest standards of financial management within the Department and leading the Commercial, Estates and Support Services, Corporate Finance, Government Banking, Change Assurance & Investment and Internal Audit directorates.

The General Counsel and Solicitor is responsible for delivering legal, accountancy, tax review and litigation services to HMRC.

The work of the HMRC Legal Group includes:

  • advising HMRC on all aspects of the law
  • carrying out the department’s civil litigation
  • providing legal and accountancy advice on policy initiatives
  • working on new legislation, such as the Finance Bill and other Bills, and also secondary legislation

Non-Executive Directors bring external experience and expertise to the department, playing an important role in providing advice, challenge and scrutiny to the work of the Executive Committee and the department more widely, both within and outside of formal Board and sub-committee meetings.

Non-Executives contribute their expertise outside the formal Board and subcommittee structures — for example, working closely with executives on specific initiatives, mentoring and undertaking in-depth examinations of risk.

Non-Executive Directors also support the effectiveness of programme boards for HMRC’s most significant transformation programmes.

Non-Executive Directors bring external experience and expertise to the department, playing an important role in providing advice, challenge and scrutiny to the work of the Executive Committee and the department more widely, both within and outside of formal Board and sub-committee meetings.

Non-Executives contribute their expertise outside the formal Board and subcommittee structures — for example, working closely with executives on specific initiatives, mentoring and undertaking in-depth examinations of risk.

Non-Executive Directors also support the effectiveness of programme boards for HMRC’s most significant transformation programmes.

Non-Executive Directors bring external experience and expertise to the department, playing an important role in providing advice, challenge and scrutiny to the work of the Executive Committee and the department more widely, both within and outside of formal Board and sub-committee meetings.

Non-Executives contribute their expertise outside the formal Board and subcommittee structures — for example, working closely with executives on specific initiatives, mentoring and undertaking in-depth examinations of risk.

Non-Executive Directors also support the effectiveness of programme boards for HMRC’s most significant transformation programmes.

HMRC’s Executive Chair and Permanent Secretary chairs the HMRC Board which in turn is responsible for shaping and developing the department’s strategy and, as HMRC’s most senior tax professional, oversees the department’s relationship with HM Treasury and provides tax policy advice to ministers.

The Director General Transformation, is the Senior Responsible Owner (SRO) for the portfolio of major programmes that together will fulfil critical elements of HMRC’s transformation necessary to secure our future as a leading example of a digitally focused tax authority, to maximise revenues, improve the customer experience and create sustainable cost reductions.

HMRC’s Chief Digital and Information Officer (CDIO) is responsible for one of the largest and most complex IT estates in the UK, and leads the department’s ambitious plans to digitalise its business.

The CDIO leads HMRC’s digital and data strategies and has significant involvement in the cross government agenda. The CDIO is also responsible for the running of live IT operations and implementing the IT Sourcing Strategy.

The CDIO is the Senior Information Risk Owner (SIRO) which covers all aspects of protective, physical, personnel and information security.

ordered_roles.title: Interim Chief Digital and Information Officer

details.body:
The Chief People Officer is responsible for the effective and efficient running of the HR Community in HM Revenue and Customs. The HR Community have accountability for people management capability and deployment across HMRC, and oversee the delivery of HR services. They are accountable for developing the HMRC People Strategy and the Employer Brand, as well as the delivery of a specialist organisation design service.

The Chief People Officer is also accountable for HMRC’s role as a responsible corporate organisation; and for the governance and delivery of HMRC’s learning and talent activities.

The Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design has overall responsibility for a group of teams that use customer insight to design better tax strategy, assurance, policies, products and processes that:

  • meet customer needs
  • make it easier for them to get things right
  • reduce the risk of honest mistakes and opportunities to dodge tax

The Director General will also advise ministers on UK tax policy, and work internationally to improve the global tax framework, while producing the strategy for all of HMRC’s core functions, not just its tax operation.

ordered_roles.title: Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design

details.body:
The Director General Customer Services has overall responsibility for delivering consistency in HMRC’s customer services and for providing customers with the joined-up support they expect, across all channels, and in ways that best suit them.

The Customer Services Group manages most of HMRC’s interaction with individual taxpayers, tax credit recipients, and small and mid-sized businesses.

The Director General Customer Compliance focuses on closing the tax gap to align with our strategic objectives by promoting voluntary compliance, preventing non-compliance and tackling those who cheat the system.

The Customer Compliance Group includes risk and intelligence, compliance for all customer groups, including large business, wealthy people, mid-sized businesses, small businesses and individuals, and HMRC’s counter–avoidance and fraud investigation functions.

The Director General Customer Services has overall responsibility for delivering consistency in HMRC’s customer services and for providing customers with the joined-up support they expect, across all channels, and in ways that best suit them.

The Customer Services Group manages most of HMRC’s interaction with individual taxpayers, tax credit recipients, and small and mid-sized businesses.

The Director General Customer Compliance focuses on minimising the tax gap by promoting voluntary compliance, preventing non-compliance and tackling those who cheat the system.

The Customer Compliance Group includes risk and intelligence, compliance for all customer groups, including large business, wealthy people, mid-sized businesses, small businesses and individuals, and HMRC’s counter–avoidance and fraud investigation functions.

This is a new cross government role, designed specifically to co-ordinate and assure all the work which is necessary to ensure UK borders continue to be effective and efficient, as we exit the EU in 2019, and continue to protect security, revenue and facilitate all cross border traffic and trade.

The role will also lead on and co-ordinate the longer term work to deliver a more “frictionless” border, post exit.

The Deputy Chief Executive and Second Permanent Secretary leads 50,000 colleagues in HMRC who deliver customer service and compliance and enforcement activity across all taxes. In addition, the Deputy Chief Executive is accountable for the transformation and change programmes which ensure the implementation of government policy and a modern, digital tax system.

The Deputy Chief Executive is a Tax Commissioner and accountable to Parliament for the effective and efficient running of HMRC.

ordered_roles.title: Deputy Chief Executive and Second Permanent Secretary

details.body:
The Interim Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design has overall responsibility for a group of teams who use customer insight to design better tax strategy, assurance, policies, products and processes that:

  • meet customer needs
  • make it easier for them to get things right
  • reduce the risk of honest mistakes and opportunities to dodge tax

The Director General will also advise ministers on UK tax policy, and work internationally to improve the global tax framework, while producing the strategy for all of HMRC’s core functions, not just its tax operation.

ordered_roles.title: Interim Director General Customer Strategy and Tax Design

details.body: The Director General Strategy leads on developing HMRC’s strategy and the next wave of reform to deliver it, including planning for the next Spending Review.

The General Counsel and Solicitor is responsible for all legal services to HMRC, whether provided directly from Solicitor’s Office or from the private sector.

The work of Solicitor’s Office includes the provision of legal advice to the department; work on new legislation, such as the Finance Bill and other Bills, and also subordinate legislation; and the department’s civil litigation. They are also responsible for corporate governance in the department and for the work of the Commissioner’s Advisory Accountant.

Non-executive directors bring external experience and expertise to the department, playing an important role in providing advice, challenge and scrutiny to the work of the executive and the department more widely, both within and outside formal board and sub-committee meetings.

Non-executives contribute their expertise outside the formal board and sub-committee structures — for example, working closely with executives on specific initiatives and mentoring, and undertaking in-depth examinations of risk.

Non-executive directors also support the effectiveness of programme boards for HMRC’s most significant transformation programmes.

HM Revenue & Customs’ (HMRC’s) Interim Chief Executive chairs the Executive Committee, and is responsible for delivering the department’s strategy, business objectives and performance.

They are the Interim Accounting Officer and are accountable to Parliament for the department’s expenditure and performance.

The Directors General for Borders and Trade have overall responsibility for the policy, design and delivery of HMRC’s accountabilities for European Union Transition.

In addition to EU transition the role has departmental responsibility for delivery of customs revenue at the border, working closely with Border Force.

The Directors General for Borders and Trade have overall responsibility for the policy, design and delivery of HMRC’s accountabilities for European Union Transition.

In addition to EU transition the role has departmental responsibility for delivery of customs revenue at the border, working closely with Border Force.

ordered_roles.title: Interim Directors General Borders and Trade

details.body: The Director General COVID-19 Response Unit is responsible for overseeing HMRC’s response to the coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic. This includes delivering the flagship support schemes, and the governance and departmental strategy relating to COVID-19.

The role involves:

  • directly delivering our major cross-cutting programmes while ensuring collaboration across HMRC’s other business groups
  • exercising oversight and co-ordination of the portfolio, both the major changes and the range of projects that will continue to be delivered from other business groups
  • leading HMRC’s project delivery function, setting standards and ensuring project professionals are trained, developed and perform to those standards

The Director General Borders and Trade has overall responsibility for leading and delivering the transformation of post EU Exit border and customs arrangements and facilitating trade with international partners.

The role also has departmental responsibility for delivery of customs revenue at the border, working closely with Border Force.

The Director General Borders and Trade has overall responsibility for leading and delivering the transformation of post EU Exit border and customs arrangements and facilitating trade with international partners.

The role also has departmental responsibility for delivery of customs revenue at the border, working closely with Border Force.

ordered_roles.title: Interim Director General Borders and Trade

The Chief People Officer (interim) is responsible for the effective and efficient running of the HR Community in HM Revenue and Customs.

The HR community has accountability for people management capability and deployment across HMRC, and oversees the delivery of HR services.

It is accountable for developing the HMRC People Strategy and the Employer Brand, as well as the delivery of a specialist organisation design service.

The Chief People Officer (interim) is also accountable for HMRC’s role as a responsible corporate organisation and for the governance and delivery of HMRC’s learning and talent activities.
The Director General for HMRC’s Change Delivery group manages the departments change portfolio and leads on some of the largest and most complex change programmes.
